title: To Virgilius, Bishop of Arelate (Arles).

source.title: NPNF-213. Gregory the Great (II), Ephraim Syrus, Aphrahat

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Since by the testimony of Holy Writ avarice is called the service of idols, with what earnestness it ought to be banished from the temple of God is acknowledged; and yet (we say it with groaning) by some priests this is not regarded.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For fierce cupidity holds the heart captive, and persuades one that what it commands is lawful, and so proceeds as to slay with the same sword both the giver and the receiver.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: What safe place, then, can hereafter be of avail against avarice, if the Church of God is opened to it by bad priests?

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: How can he keep the sheepfolds inviolate who invites the wolf to enter?

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: He pollutes his hands by an unlawful bribe, and thinks to lift up others by his benediction, while himself prostrate under his own iniquity, and captive notwithstanding to his own ambition.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Since then this evil of rapacity has never entered the citadel of your mind, and you say that you have your hands unpolluted in the matter of ordinations, give thanks to Almighty God, and acknowledge yourselves to be His debtors in that under His protection you have remained unharmed by the contagion of this disease.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: But this good in you will profit you less than it might have done if you have not carefully forbidden this thing in others also.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: As in thyself this evil had displeased thee, thou oughtest to have been zealous against it in thy brother also.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: For, seeing that the divine precepts admonish us to love our neighbours as ourselves, it is no small fault to disregard them, and not to fear for others what for ourselves we shrink from.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Even now, therefore, most beloved brother, give thy mind to repairing what thou hast lost in others through thy negligence in correction, and restrain whomsoever thou canst from this wickedness, and insist on a synod being assembled for rooting out this same heresy, to the end that, with reward to thy Love, what shall have been condemned, God granting it, by the ordinance of all may be better guarded against by all.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Furthermore, it has come to our ears that our brother and fellow-bishop, Serenus of Massilia (Marseilles), receives bad men into his intimate society, so as to have, in fine, as his familiar friend a certain presbyter. who, after lapse, is said to wallow still in his iniquities.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: This you ought to enquire into closely.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: And, if it should prove to be so, let it be your care so to correct this matter in our stead that both he who has received such a one may learn not to encourage him by familiarity, but rather to constrain him by punishment, and he who has been received may learn to wash away his sins with tears, and not to pile up iniquity by unclean living.

verses.book: Selected Epistles of Gregory the Great.
verses.text: Let your Fraternity hold as commended to you in all respects the monks whom we have sent to our brother and fellow-bishop Augustine, and take pains so to succour them for proceeding on their way, and so to concur with them, that through your assistance they may be able, under the protection of God, to arrive speedily at their destination.
